description: Fix three classes of bugs in the MCP server's dev-mode infrastructure: (1) dev-proxy.ts has a session memory leak (sessions not evicted on client disconnect), a splice(-1,1) bug that drops a pending request, and a module-level race in pendingWsProtocol; (2) dev-proxy.ts also fails to restart the worker after an unexpected crash, hanging the proxy indefinitely; (3) file-watcher.ts drops hot-reload events on Linux because fs.watch can pass null as the filename argument, and the EMFILE escalation to polling fails when the mtime poll timer hasn't started yet.

userStories.title: Fix dev-proxy session memory leak — evict sessions on SSE stream close with a TTL
userStories.description: In dev-proxy.ts, MCP sessions are created on `POST /mcp initialize` and deleted only on `DELETE /mcp`. If a client crashes or disconnects without sending DELETE, the session is never cleaned up. The comment at line 616–619 intentionally avoids cleanup on SSE close to support clients that can re-open the stream. Add a TTL-based eviction: if no SSE stream is active for a session for 10 minutes, evict it.
acceptanceCriteria: Sessions without an active `upstreamSse` connection for more than 10 minutes are automatically evicted via `cleanupSession`
Active sessions (with a live SSE connection) are never evicted by the TTL
Sessions are still cleaned up immediately on explicit `DELETE /mcp` as before
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/dev-proxy.ts

Context:
The `sessions` Map (line 78) maps proxySessionId → ProxySession. `cleanupSession` (lines 187–199) removes and tears down a session. Sessions are only cleaned up via `DELETE /mcp` (line 677) or failed `reinitializeSessions` (lines 124, 131, 181).

The comment at line 616–619: 'Sessions are cleaned up only via explicit DELETE /mcp or when the proxy restarts.'

Design for TTL eviction:
1. Add an `idleSince: number | null` field to `ProxySession` — set to `Date.now()` when `disconnectUpstreamSse` is called, cleared when `connectUpstreamSse` succeeds
2. Add a periodic sweep (e.g., `setInterval(() => sweepIdleSessions(), 60_000)`) that iterates `sessions` and calls `cleanupSession` for entries where `idleSince !== null && Date.now() - idleSince > SESSION_IDLE_TTL_MS` (10 minutes)
3. Clear the sweep interval on proxy shutdown

Alternative (simpler): set a per-session timeout when `disconnectUpstreamSse` fires; clear it on `connectUpstreamSse`:
```typescript
const SESSION_IDLE_TTL_MS = 10 * 60 * 1000;
if (session.idleTimer) clearTimeout(session.idleTimer);
session.idleTimer = setTimeout(() => cleanupSession(session.proxySessionId), SESSION_IDLE_TTL_MS);
```

Constraints:
- Read the full ProxySession interface before adding fields
- The idle timer must be cancelled in `cleanupSession` to prevent double-cleanup
- Do NOT clean up sessions when sseStreams empty — that is intentional (clients can reconnect)
- Only trigger idle tracking when the upstream SSE connection (not client streams) becomes idle

userStories.title: Fix dev-proxy splice(-1,1) bug, worker-crash no-restart, and pendingWsProtocol race
userStories.description: Three bugs in dev-proxy.ts: (a) `whenReady` timeout handler calls `pending.splice(pending.indexOf(entry), 1)` — if `entry` was already drained, `indexOf` returns -1 and `splice(-1, 1)` removes the last pending entry. (b) Worker crash after becoming ready sets `workerPort = null` but does not restart the worker, leaving the proxy hanging. (c) `pendingWsProtocol` is a module-level variable shared across concurrent WebSocket upgrades.
acceptanceCriteria: In `whenReady`, the timeout handler guards `indexOf` result: `const idx = pending.indexOf(entry); if (idx >= 0) pending.splice(idx, 1);`
The `child.on('exit')` handler in `startWorker` calls `startWorker()` to restart the process when it exits unexpectedly (not from a planned kill)
`pendingWsProtocol` is no longer a shared module-level variable; the connectionId is passed through per-upgrade scope

userStories.title: Fix file-watcher null filename on Linux and EMFILE escalation failure
userStories.description: In file-watcher.ts, two bugs prevent reliable hot reload in edge cases: (a) `fs.watch` callbacks receive `filename: string | null` on Linux — direct comparison `filename === TOOLS_FILENAME` without a null guard silently skips the event; (b) the EMFILE fast-poll escalation in `startConfigWatching` checks `fw.mtimePollTimerId !== null` but this condition is false if called before `startMtimePolling` has run, silently leaving config changes undetected.
acceptanceCriteria: In `watchPlugin` and `watchPendingPlugin`, `fs.watch` callbacks guard `filename` with a null check before comparing to `TOOLS_FILENAME` or `ADAPTER_FILENAME`; when filename is null, the handler falls back to re-reading both files
In `startConfigWatching`, the EMFILE escalation path (`catch (err)` block) triggers mtime polling regardless of whether it was already running
